content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom ___ (sleep) when his alarm went off at seven o'clock.
distractor_rationale: past simple describes a completed action, but sleeping was the ongoing background activity interrupted by the alarm
was + base form is grammatically incorrect; the continuous requires was + verb-ing
present continuous cannot be used for a past moment

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The children ___ (play) in the garden all morning while their mother worked inside.
distractor_rationale: past simple cannot describe a continuous background activity paired with 'all morning while'
were + base form is grammatically incorrect; the continuous requires were + verb-ing
present continuous cannot refer to a past time frame

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Maria dropped her bag when she ___ (run) to catch the bus.
distractor_rationale: past simple 'ran' would make two equal completed actions, but running was the ongoing background to the sudden drop
present simple cannot refer to a past event
was run is a passive construction and is grammatically incorrect here

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: We ___ (have) dinner at the kitchen table when the electricity suddenly went out.
distractor_rationale: past simple 'had' describes a completed event, but the dinner was an ongoing activity interrupted by the power cut
present perfect cannot be used for a past moment interrupted by another past event
were had is a passive construction and is grammatically incorrect in this context

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: James was reading the report when his manager ___ (walk) into the office and asked him a question.
distractor_rationale: past continuous is for the background action; the manager's entry is the sudden interrupting event, which takes past simple
present simple cannot refer to a completed past action
past perfect implies the walking was completed before another past event, but it is the interrupting action here

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: At nine o'clock last night, Lisa ___ (study) for her exam while her flatmate watched television.
distractor_rationale: past simple cannot describe an activity in progress at a specific past moment indicated by 'at nine o'clock last night'
present perfect cannot refer to an activity in progress at a specific past clock time
was + base form is grammatically incorrect; the continuous form requires was + verb-ing

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: While Anna ___ (wait) for her friend outside the café, it started to rain heavily.
distractor_rationale: past simple 'waited' describes a completed action, not an ongoing background state interrupted by the rain
present simple cannot be used to describe a past situation
present perfect continuous cannot refer to a past moment before another past event

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The engineer noticed the fault while she ___ (check) the equipment on the factory floor that afternoon.
distractor_rationale: past simple 'checked' implies a completed sequence rather than an ongoing activity during which the noticing occurred
past perfect continuous emphasises duration before a past reference point, which is not the meaning here; the checking was simply in progress when the fault was noticed
was checked is a passive construction and changes the meaning entirely

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: David heard a loud noise outside, so he looked out of the window; two workers ___ (repair) the road directly in front of his building.
distractor_rationale: past simple 'repaired' describes a completed event, but the repair work was an ongoing background scene David observed at that moment
past perfect 'had repaired' implies the work was already finished before David looked, which contradicts the scene he was observing
were repaired is a passive construction implying the road was the object, not the workers performing the action

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Sara realised she had left her keys at home only after she ___ (arrive) at the office and tried to open her desk drawer.
distractor_rationale: past continuous 'was arriving' implies arrival was an extended background process, but arriving at the office is a single completed point-in-time event in this sequence
past perfect continuous 'had been arriving' is grammatically awkward and semantically impossible; arrival is a momentary event, not a duration
present perfect 'has arrived' cannot be used in a completed past sequence anchored to a past time

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom ___ (sleep) when his alarm went off at seven o'clock this morning.
distractor_rationale: past simple describes a completed action, not a background action in progress at the moment the alarm went off
was + base form is a common formation error; the correct structure requires was + verb-ing
present continuous cannot be used for a past moment; the whole scene is set in the past

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The children ___ (play) in the garden all afternoon while their parents prepared dinner inside.
distractor_rationale: past simple signals a completed event, but 'all afternoon' marks a continuous background activity running alongside the parents' actions
were + base form is a formation error; the past continuous requires were + verb-ing
present continuous cannot refer to a past time frame; the sentence is clearly set in the past

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Maria ___ (walk) to the bus stop when she suddenly noticed her bag was missing.
distractor_rationale: past simple describes a completed action; here the walking was an ongoing background activity interrupted by the sudden noticing
past perfect describes an action completed before another past event, but the walking was still in progress when the noticing occurred
present perfect continuous cannot be used for a completed past sequence entirely set in past time

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: While Lisa was checking her emails at the office, her colleague ___ (print) the reports for the afternoon meeting.
distractor_rationale: past simple signals a completed action, but the printing was a simultaneous background activity running alongside Lisa's checking, as shown by 'while'
the subject 'her colleague' is singular, so 'were' is incorrect; was printing is required
past perfect indicates an action completed before another past action, but both activities were happening at the same time

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: At exactly nine o'clock last night, James and Sara ___ (discuss) the project results when the power cut out and the lights went off.
distractor_rationale: past simple describes a completed sequence of events, but the discussion was an ongoing activity in progress at the specific moment the power cut out
past perfect continuous emphasises duration before a past event and would imply the discussion had already ended when the power cut out, contradicting the interruption structure
were discussed is passive voice and changes the meaning entirely, making James and Sara the recipients of discussion rather than the people doing it

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The children ___ in the garden when it started to rain.
distractor_rationale: past simple describes a completed action, not an ongoing background activity interrupted by 'it started to rain'
was/were + base form is a common learner error; the correct form requires the -ing participle: were playing
present continuous cannot describe a past background scene; the context is clearly in the past

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom ___ a shower when his phone rang loudly.
distractor_rationale: past simple 'had' describes a completed event, not an action in progress at the moment the phone rang
'was have' incorrectly uses the base form after 'was'; the -ing form 'having' is required for past continuous
present perfect 'has had' cannot locate an action at a specific past moment; it also uses the wrong tense entirely

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: At eight o'clock last night, Maria ___ dinner with her family.
distractor_rationale: past simple 'ate' describes a completed action, but the time marker 'at eight o'clock last night' signals an action in progress at that specific moment
present continuous 'is eating' cannot describe a past moment; the time marker 'last night' demands a past tense
'was ate' incorrectly combines the past continuous auxiliary 'was' with the past simple form; the -ing participle 'eating' is required

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: She ___ a letter to her friend when the electricity suddenly went off.
distractor_rationale: past simple 'wrote' describes a completed action; the context requires an ongoing activity that was interrupted by the sudden power cut
past perfect continuous 'has been writing' is incorrect here because it requires 'had been writing'; it also shifts focus to duration before a past event rather than the moment of interruption
present continuous 'is writing' cannot describe a past scene; the time frame is clearly past as shown by 'went off'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: James was watching a film when his neighbour ___ the door loudly.
distractor_rationale: 'was knocking' incorrectly puts the interrupting action in the continuous form; the sudden, completed interruption ('knocked') must use the past simple
present perfect 'has knocked' cannot describe a specific completed past event that interrupted another action; it also conflicts with the past tense of the main clause
present simple 'knocks' cannot describe a past event; the sentence is entirely set in the past

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: When we arrived at the café, Anna and David ___ at a table near the window.
distractor_rationale: past simple 'sat' implies they sat down at that moment of arrival, but the context describes a background scene already in progress when we arrived
'were sit' incorrectly uses the base form after 'were'; past continuous requires the -ing participle: were sitting
past perfect 'had sat' describes an action completed before another past action; here the activity (sitting) was still ongoing at the moment of arrival, so past continuous is needed

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The manager ___ on the phone when the fire alarm went off in the building.
distractor_rationale: past simple 'talked' describes a completed action, but the context requires an ongoing activity that was interrupted by the fire alarm
'was talked' confuses past continuous with passive voice; the subject (the manager) is the agent performing the action, not receiving it
'has been talking' is present perfect continuous and cannot locate an action at a specific past moment; the past simple 'went off' anchors the scene in the past

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: While Lisa ___ the report for tomorrow's meeting, her colleague sent her several urgent emails.
distractor_rationale: past simple 'prepared' would describe a completed action, but 'while' signals that this was an ongoing background activity during which the emails arrived
'had been preparing' (past perfect continuous) emphasises duration completed before another past event; here the focus is on the ongoing activity at the moment of interruption, not duration before it
'was prepared' is a passive construction implying someone else prepared Lisa, which is semantically wrong in this context

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The guests were chatting quietly and ___ their drinks when the host suddenly announced the winner.
distractor_rationale: past simple 'enjoyed' breaks the parallel structure; the sentence already has 'were' governing both verbs ('were chatting and ___ their drinks'), so the second verb needs only the -ing form to complete the compound past continuous

'were enjoying' is grammatically possible in isolation but creates awkward repetition of the auxiliary 'were' in a coordinated structure where the auxiliary is already shared; the -ing participle alone is the correct parallel form here
past perfect 'had enjoyed' shifts the time frame to before the chatting and cannot be coordinated with 'were chatting' to describe simultaneous background actions

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: By the time Mark reached the station, the last train ___ and the platform was completely empty.
distractor_rationale: 'was leaving' (past continuous) describes an action in progress at a past moment, but the context shows the train's departure was fully completed before Mark arrived; past perfect is required to express this prior completion
past simple 'left' does not clearly signal that the departure happened before Mark's arrival; the sequence of events requires past perfect to show one past action was completed before another
present perfect 'has left' cannot be used in a past narrative context; the time frame is anchored in the past by 'reached', requiring past perfect 'had left'

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The children played in the garden when it started to rain.
content.error_sentence: The children played in the garden when it started to rain.
content.corrected_sentence: The children were playing in the garden when it started to rain.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: She was read a book on the sofa when her phone rang.
content.error_sentence: She was read a book on the sofa when her phone rang.
content.corrected_sentence: She was reading a book on the sofa when her phone rang.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom was cooking dinner when his sister was arrived home from school.
content.error_sentence: Tom was cooking dinner when his sister was arrived home from school.
content.corrected_sentence: Tom was cooking dinner when his sister arrived home from school.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: I was cooking and eating lunch before I went back to the office.
content.error_sentence: I was cooking and eating lunch before I went back to the office.
content.corrected_sentence: I cooked and ate lunch before I went back to the office.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Mark listened to music in his room when his mother called him for dinner.
content.error_sentence: Mark listened to music in his room when his mother called him for dinner.
content.corrected_sentence: Mark was listening to music in his room when his mother called him for dinner.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: When the fire alarm went off, most of the students sat quietly at their desks.
content.error_sentence: When the fire alarm went off, most of the students sat quietly at their desks.
content.corrected_sentence: When the fire alarm went off, most of the students were sitting quietly at their desks.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Anna was walking to the bus stop when she was realising she had left her bag at home.
content.error_sentence: Anna was walking to the bus stop when she was realising she had left her bag at home.
content.corrected_sentence: Anna was walking to the bus stop when she realised she had left her bag at home.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The manager was presenting the results to the team when the projector was suddenly stopping.
content.error_sentence: The manager was presenting the results to the team when the projector was suddenly stopping.
content.corrected_sentence: The manager was presenting the results to the team when the projector suddenly stopped.
